A&W finds new meanings behind its name
By Laurie Wilson
January 30, 2026
With a presence in Canada that stretches back to 1956, the names behind A&W’s initialism may have been lost to time for many who have sampled the QSR’s trademark products. A new campaign from Rethink leans into that bit of trivia to show that the customers’ connection to brand go far beyond the names of the restaurant’s founders.
A 30-second hero spot anchors the launch, spotlighting A&W’s Teen Burger and reinforcing what the chain truly “stands for” in the minds of Canadians. The commercial focuses on the conversations of curious customers who put forth their own theories, like “amazing and wild,” “awesome and wonderful” and “‘amburgers and woot beer.”
While few may know what the letters A&W literally represent – Roy W. Allen and Frank Wright, who began franchising A&W in California in the 1920s – the campaign reframes the acronym as a shorthand for the values and products behind the brand.
“We’re returning our focus to what we do best: burgers and taste,” A&W Canada’s VP of marketing, Tom Newitt, said in a news release. “This campaign uses a striking creative lens to celebrate exactly what people love most about the A&W brand.”
Visually, the work integrates the A&W logo through superimposed text to bridge brand recognition with its grass-fed-beef ingredient differentiation, spelling out what the brand stands for by embedding type onto napkins and custom neon signage like “A&Www yeah” and “A&Wesome.”
The campaign also marks the introduction of a refreshed A&W visual identity, developed in partnership with Rethink, the brand’s creative partner since 2000.
The “Stands for What’s Good” campaign extends across 15-second and six-second cutdowns and is backed by a paid-media strategy that includes a primetime Canadian spot during the Super Bowl on Feb. 8. Launched Thursday, the campaign is rolling out across OOH, social and digital channels with support from Rethink PR and iPro on media.
